title: Unintended Policy Shifts and Unexpected Consequences
summary: Though the bulk of this speech is on bank capital and the supplementary leverage ratio's distortion of Treasury market intermediation, it opens with a substantive monetary-policy section that marks Bowman's pivot toward easing. She supported June's hold but states this is the speech where she begins to lean dovish: she sees inflation on a sustained path to 2 percent (core PCE 2.6 percent, tariff effects likely small, delayed, and one-off) and growing downside risks to employment from softer demand and emerging labor-market fragility, even as she still describes the labor market as solid. She explicitly signals she would support a cut as soon as the July meeting if inflation pressures stay contained. The read is a mildly dovish shift from a still-restrictive stance.

key_quotes.quote: Should inflation pressures remain contained, I would support lowering the policy rate as soon as our next meeting in July in order to bring it closer to its neutral setting and to sustain a healthy labor market.
key_quotes.context: Path forward in the monetary-policy section; first explicit signal she is ready to cut.

key_quotes.quote: With inflation on a sustained trajectory toward 2 percent, softness in aggregate demand, and signs of fragility in the labor market, I think that we should put more weight on downside risks to our employment mandate going forward.
key_quotes.context: Shifting the balance of risks toward employment.

key_quotes.quote: The labor market appears to be stable near estimates of full employment, with layoffs remaining low.
key_quotes.context: Current labor market description, still characterized as solid in June.

key_quotes.quote: I think it is likely that the impact of tariffs on inflation may take longer, be more delayed, and have a smaller effect than initially expected, especially because many firms front-loaded their stocks of inventories.
